skill.name: follow-up
skill.description: Write follow-up emails that re-engage without being annoying. Uses proven psychology for follow-up sequences. 42% of replies come from follow-ups.
skill.summary: Generate professional follow-up emails using psychology-backed frameworks that increase response rates.
skill.author: humanizerai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected two potential issues that are both false positives. The 'blocker' finding incorrectly flagged line 3 as containing weak cryptography (it is only a description field). The 'external_commands' finding incorrectly flagged markdown code blocks at lines 76-82 as shell backtick execution (they are formatting delimiters showing email output format). This is a pure writing/documentation skill with no executable code, network access, or security risks.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Block Detected as Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer detected markdown backtick syntax at lines 76-82 and flagged it as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are markdown code block delimiters showing the email output format example, not executable commands. No security risk exists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct examination of lines 76-82 shows a markdown code block containing email template text, not shell commands. The backticks are markdown syntax, not execution operators.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection Error
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged line 3 for weak cryptography. Line 3 contains only a description field about follow-up emails with no cryptographic content. This is a scanner error, not a security issue.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains only text description with no cryptographic references. This is a clear scanner false positive.

content.value_statement: Most sales opportunities die from silence, not rejection. This skill helps you write follow-up emails that re-engage prospects without being annoying using psychology-backed frameworks.
actual_capabilities: Generate follow-up emails for any follow-up number (1, 2, or 3)
Apply three frameworks: The Bump, The Value-Add, and The Breakup
Adapt tone and length based on follow-up sequence position
Include new value propositions or angles without repeating content
Create permission-based closure emails that respect recipient time
limitations: Cannot send emails directly - only generates text content
Requires manual input of original email context for best results
Does not track email open rates or response metrics
Templates follow general best practices but may need customization for specific industries

use_cases.title: Sales Prospecting
use_cases.description: Re-engage leads who did not respond to initial outreach with strategic follow-ups that add value instead of just checking in.
use_cases.target_user: Sales representatives and business development professionals

use_cases.title: Job Application Follow-Up
use_cases.description: Send professional follow-up emails after job interviews or application submissions to stay on hiring managers' radars.

use_cases.title: Client Communication
use_cases.description: Follow up on proposals, invoices, or pending decisions with clients while maintaining professional relationships.

prompt_templates.title: First Follow-Up
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a follow-up #1 email for this context: [paste original email]. It was sent 4 days ago. Add a new angle about [specific value proposition].
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you have not received a response to your initial email within 3-5 days.

prompt_templates.title: Value-Add Follow-Up
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a follow-up #2 email that shares this insight: [paste article or insight]. Reference original discussion about [topic]. Keep it under 50 words.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for your second follow-up when you want to provide additional value.

prompt_templates.title: Break-Up Email
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a follow-up #3 breakup email for [proportunity/project]. Give them an easy out and make it clear this is the last email.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for your final follow-up after 14+ days to close the loop professionally.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Bump Follow-Up
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a short bump follow-up email. Original subject was [subject]. Just float it back to the top of their inbox with a friendly tone.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want a light touch follow-up that assumes they are busy, not uninterested.

output_examples.input: Write a follow-up #1 email. Original: 'Hi, I wanted to discuss our SEO services that can increase your traffic.' Sent 4 days ago.
output_examples.output:
Subject: Quick idea regarding your traffic

Saw this article about Google's latest update and thought of our conversation about SEO.

The timing might be better than we discussed - want to explore a quick pilot?

[Your name]

Framework used: The Value-Add
What's different: Added timely industry news as hook
Word count: 42

output_examples.input: Write a follow-up #3 breakup email. Context: Proposed website redesign project.
output_examples.output:
Subject: Should I close your file?

Hi [Name],

I haven't heard back on the website redesign proposal, so I'm assuming this isn't a priority right now.

I'll close your file and free up the slot we discussed.

If circumstances change, feel free to reach out.

Best,
[Your name]

Framework used: The Breakup
What's different: Permission-based close with no pressure
Word count: 48

best_practices: Always reference the original email or conversation briefly to provide context
Keep each follow-up shorter than the previous email - follow-ups should be scannable
Add new value, insights, or angles instead of repeating the same message
Space follow-ups appropriately: #1 after 3-5 days, #2 after 7-10 days, #3 after 14+ days
anti_patterns: Avoid apologetic language like 'I'm sure you're busy but...' - it sounds weak
Never send the exact same email twice without adding new context or value
Do not use guilt-trip phrases like 'I haven't heard back' or 'Why didn't you respond?'
Avoid passive-aggressive tone about lack of response - always assume positive intent

faq.question: What is the ideal time between follow-up emails?
faq.answer: Send follow-up #1 after 3-5 days, follow-up #2 after 7-10 days, and follow-up #3 after 14+ days. This gives prospects time to respond without being forgotten or overwhelmed.

faq.question: How long should follow-up emails be?
faq.answer: Keep follow-ups between 30-75 words. They should be shorter than your original email and easily scannable. Long follow-ups reduce response rates.

faq.question: What is the breakup email framework?
faq.answer: The breakup email is your third and final follow-up. It gives prospects an easy way to say they are not interested while creating urgency. Examples include 'Should I close your file?' or 'Is this project still on hold?'

faq.question: Should I change the subject line in follow-ups?
faq.answer: For follow-ups #1 and #2, use 'Re: [original subject]' to maintain thread context. For follow-up #3 (breakup email), consider a new subject line to capture attention.

faq.question: What if I do not have anything new to add in a follow-up?
faq.answer: Use The Bump framework for a short, friendly nudge that assumes they are busy. You can also share relevant industry news, a case study, or reframe your value proposition from a different angle.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for job application follow-ups?
faq.answer: Yes. The frameworks work well for job seekers. Adapt the language to be more formal and focus on your enthusiasm for the role rather than sales language.
